title: Palette Generator
shortDescription: Create coordinated color sets from one base color for interfaces, branding, and concept work.
metaDescription: Create coordinated color sets from one base color for branding, interfaces, theme planning, and design drafts.
intro: Turn one base color into a usable set of related shades, tints, and accents in seconds.
overview:
This tool helps you turn a single starting color into a structured set of related colors. That makes it easier to build interfaces with consistent surfaces, text states, and accent tones without manually guessing each step.

It is also useful when you need an early visual direction for a brand, landing page, or product concept. Instead of working with one color in isolation, you get a small family of colors that can support headers, backgrounds, highlights, and interaction states.

Use the generated values as a starting point, then fine-tune them in your design file or codebase if you need stricter contrast, warmer neutrals, or a more restrained look.
howItWorks: Pick a base color that represents the starting direction.
Choose how many related colors you want in the set.
Review the swatches and copy the values you want into your design or code.

useCases.description: Test a hero section, accent color, and supporting sections with a coherent color family before polishing the layout.

useCases.description: Expand one signature color into a palette that can be used across slides, mood boards, and visual identity sketches.

examples.title: SaaS dashboard palette
examples.output: A balanced set with pale teal backgrounds, mid-tone UI accents, and a deep anchor shade
examples.description: Works well for navigation, charts, and status highlights.

examples.title: Editorial landing page
examples.output: Soft peach tints, a saturated coral middle tone, and a darker emphasis color

examples.title: Product launch moodboard
examples.output: Several steps from near-black plum to light lavender
examples.description: Gives you enough variation for cover cards, section breaks, and feature callouts.

faq.question: Will every generated palette keep the same mood as the base color?
faq.answer: The set stays tied to the starting color, but the lighter and darker steps can feel more neutral or more vivid depending on how far they move from the base. If you want a very specific mood, check the full range and choose only the closest steps.

faq.question: Can I use the results directly in code?
faq.answer: Yes. The output is meant to be copied as color values for styles, tokens, or theme files. If your project needs strict naming, rename the swatches before adding them to your codebase.

faq.question: What if one swatch is too pale for text or buttons?
faq.answer: That usually means the generated step is better suited for backgrounds or borders. Pick a deeper shade from the same set for text and interactive elements.

faq.question: Does a larger palette always work better?
faq.answer: Not necessarily. Large sets give you more choice, but smaller sets are easier to keep consistent. For product work, it is often better to start with fewer tones and add only what your layout actually needs.
